title: Baba ganoush
subtitle: Smoked aubergine dip with tahini, lemon, and garlic.
excerpt: Whole aubergines charred over open flame until the skin blackens and the flesh collapses. The smoky flesh whisked with tahini, lemon juice, garlic, and salt. A Levantine mezze staple, served at room temperature with warm flatbread.
sourceNotes: The Levantine mezze tradition as documented in regional Lebanese and Syrian cookery. The open-flame charring method is the canonical step that separates baba ganoush from oven-roasted aubergine dips.

recipe.yieldDescription: ~350 g, enough for a generous mezze plate
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps for 2 days in the fridge. The flavour deepens overnight; bring back to room temperature before serving.
recipe.temperatureNote: open flame on the hob, or grill

content.text: Smoke is the whole point. The aubergines go directly onto a gas flame, or under a screaming-hot grill, and they char until the skin is uniformly black and the flesh inside has collapsed to a soft pulp. That char is where the dish gets its name: "baba ganoush" loosely translates as "pampered daddy", but the dish is anything but coddled. It comes off the flame looking ruined.
Drain the cooked flesh through a sieve for 15 minutes before whisking. Aubergines hold a great deal of water, and the smoke flavour is concentrated in the solids. Wet baba ganoush tastes thin.

items.prepNote: small, crushed to a paste with salt

content.text: Long-handled tongs to turn the aubergines on the flame. A fine-mesh sieve for draining the cooked flesh.

Put {{aubergine}} of aubergines directly onto a gas-hob flame on medium-high. Turn every 4 to 5 minutes with tongs until the skin is uniformly blackened and the flesh has fully collapsed under your tongs. This takes 20 to 25 minutes total. Watch the kitchen extractor and have a window open.

If you have no gas, set the oven grill to its highest setting and lay the aubergines on a tray on the top shelf. Turn every 4 minutes for 20 minutes total. The skin won't blacken in quite the same way, so a teaspoon of liquid smoke or a pinch of smoked paprika at the whisking stage compensates.

Sit the charred aubergines on a plate to cool 10 minutes. Slit each one open lengthways and scrape the soft flesh into a fine-mesh sieve over a bowl. Leave to drain for 15 minutes; press lightly with a wooden spoon. You should have around 200 g of drained flesh.

Tip the drained flesh into a medium bowl. Add {{tahini}} of tahini, the juice of {{lemon}} of lemon, {{garlic}} of crushed garlic, and {{sea-salt-flakes}} of salt flakes. Whisk vigorously with a fork or a whisk for 1 minute. The mixture will lighten and emulsify. Taste; adjust salt and lemon. The flavour should be smoky, tangy, garlicky, and creamy in equal measure.
Refrigerate for 30 minutes for the flavours to settle, then bring back to cool room temperature.

Spread on a wide shallow plate. Make a swoosh with the back of a spoon. Drizzle with {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil. Scatter with {{parsley-flat}} of chopped parsley, the sumac if using, and the pomegranate seeds. Eat with warm flatbread, raw vegetables, or as part of a mezze plate.

items.symptom: Baba ganoush tastes flat, not smoky
items.cause: Aubergine charred only on the outside; flesh not fully cooked.
items.fix: Keep going until the aubergine fully collapses under tongs. If you press and feel firmness inside, it isn't done. Use a flame, not oven roast.
Drain for the full 15 minutes in a sieve. Press lightly with a spoon to encourage the liquid out. The drained flesh should be soft but no longer pooling.

items.cause: Raw garlic added in chunks rather than as paste.
items.fix: Crush one small clove with the salt to a smooth paste before adding. This breaks down the volatile compounds and gives a gentler, more even garlic flavour.

items.cause: Black aubergine skin scraped in with the flesh.
items.fix: Take only the soft pulp; leave the charred skin behind. Small flecks are fine and add character; chunks are bitter.

content.text: Baba ganoush is a staple of mezze across Lebanon, Syria, Jordan, and Palestine. The Greek and Turkish versions, melitzanosalata and patlıcan ezmesi, sit close in spirit but vary in finish and seasoning. Serve alongside hummus, labneh, tabbouleh, and warm flatbread for a generous summer lunch.
